message.content: Indirect Command Execution allows adversaries to bypass security restrictions by leveraging utilities that execute commands without directly invoking command-line interpreters like cmd.exe. This technique can be used for Defense Evasion, enabling arbitrary command execution while avoiding detection and mitigation controls. Commonly exploited utilities include Forfiles, the Program Compatibility Assistant, and components of the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL). These tools can execute commands from scripts, the Run window, or other command and scripting interpreters. Notable examples include the Lazarus Group using file to execute .htm files and Revenge RAT utilizing Forfiles for command execution. Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and restricting the use of these utilities, implementing strict Group Policy settings, and employing behavioral analysis to detect unusual command execution patterns.
